programs.name: Medicaid Long Term Care Services and Supports (LTSS)

eligibility.income_limits: Varies by marital status and program. For Nursing Home Medicaid and HCBS Waivers (2026): Single - greater than $2,982/month (must contribute most to state); SSI pathway - Single $994/month, Couple $1,491/month. Married (one applying): specific limits apply per table in sources. ABD Medicaid: functional need for basic coverage if 65+.[1][2]
eligibility.asset_limits: Single: $2,000-$2,500 (varies by program, e.g., Assisted Living $2,000 most/$2,500 some). Married: Varies, community spouse allowance applies. Counts current/past 5 years finances; trusts may protect assets. Exempt: not detailed, standard Medicaid exemptions likely (home equity limits apply).[2][3][6]
other_requirements: Maryland resident
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) for Nursing Home/HCBS: inability in ADLs (mobility, bathing, etc.) and IADLs (shopping, etc.), cognitive/behavioral issues.[1][2][4]
Functional assessment for services; disability if under 65.[3][5]

benefits.value: Home/community-based: personal assistance, nursing, nurse monitoring, medical day care, case management, transportation, medical supplies/equipment. Institutional: nursing facilities, intermediate care for intellectual disabilities. Fee-for-service, not managed care. Targets 65+, disabilities, dual eligibles; based on medical necessity.[5]

methods: Online: health.maryland.gov/mmcp/eligibility/Pages/Home.aspx[9]
Phone: not specified in results, contact Maryland Department of Health
In-person: local offices (e.g., Montgomery County HHS)[3]
Mail: via Maryland Department of Health
application.waitlist: Not detailed; may exist for HCBS waivers
forms: Medical Assistance application via Maryland eligibility site[9]

geography.regional_variations: Local offices handle (e.g., Montgomery County); potential wait time/provider differences by region not detailed[3]
offices_or_providers: Maryland Department of Health; local health departments

gotchas: Income >$2,982/month requires paying most to state (not upper limit)[1]
5-year lookback on finances[3]
NFLOC required for institutional/HCBS, not all regular Medicaid LTSS[2]
Asset protection via trusts complex[7]
Must prove citizenship/identity[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Varies by marital status/program (Nursing Home vs HCBS vs ABD); NFLOC tiered need; 5-year financial history review; statewide but local admin

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (includes SLMB and QI programs)

eligibility.income_limits: Must be a Medicare Part A recipient. Income based on federal poverty level (FPL) percentages, varying by program tier and household size (primarily individual or couple). Maryland follows federal standards with $20 general income disregard. For 2026: SLMB (100-120% FPL): Individual up to ~$1,526/month, couple up to ~$2,064/month[1][4][5]; QI/SLMB II (120-135% FPL): Individual up to ~$1,660-$1,715/month, couple up to ~$2,239-$2,320/month[1][4][5]. Exact limits adjust annually; check current FPL via Medicare.gov. Older sources show lower 2021 figures (SLMB individual $1,308, couple $1,762; QI individual $1,469, couple $1,980)[2].
eligibility.asset_limits: Federal limits apply: $9,090-$9,950 individual, $13,630-$14,910 couple (varies slightly by source/year; 2024 examples $9,430/$14,130)[1][4][5][6]. Countable: cash, bank accounts, stocks, bonds. Exempt: primary home, one car, burial plot, up to $1,500 burial expenses, life insurance < $1,500 cash value, furniture, household/personal items[2].
other_requirements: Must have Medicare Part A (and Part B to qualify)
Maryland resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Net countable income within tier limits per Md. Code Regs. 10.09.24.03-3[3]

benefits.value: Pays monthly Medicare Part B premium (~$202.90 in 2026). SLMB and QI cover only Part B premiums; no deductibles/copays (unlike QMB)[1][2][3][4].

methods: Online: https://www.marylandhealthconnection.gov/[2]
Phone: (855) 642-8572 or TTY (855) 642-8573 (Maryland Health Benefits Exchange)[2]
Mail: Download application from Maryland Department of Human Resources Medical Assistance page; mail to local Dept. of Social Services[2]
In-person: Local department of social services office or county DHS (e.g., Montgomery County: 301-255-4250)[2][9]
application.processing_time: Current eligibility effective first day of month after determination; QI retroactive up to Jan 1 of application year if eligible prior months. No specific timeline stated; apply yearly[2][3].
application.waitlist: QI: First-come, first-served with priority to prior-year recipients; funding limited[2][4].
forms: Maryland Department of Human Resources Medical Assistance application[2]
documents: Proof of income (e.g., pay stubs, tax returns)

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide for eligible Maryland Medicare recipients[7]
geography.regional_variations: Apply via local Dept. of Social Services; county offices handle (e.g., Montgomery County specific contacts), but uniform eligibility statewide. No noted differences in benefits/providers/wait times[2][9].
offices_or_providers: Local Department of Social Services offices by county[2]

gotchas: QI funding limited—apply early in year; first-come, first-served with prior recipients prioritized[2][4]
Must reapply every year[2]
Must have Part A and B; only covers Part B premium, not other costs[2][3]
Income/assets counted strictly; exemptions often missed (e.g., home/car exempt)[2]
Limits based on FPL—adjust annually; outdated figures (e.g., 2021) lead to errors[1][2]
SLMB called base, QI as 'SLMB II' in MD[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by FPL (SLMB 100-120%, QI 120-135%); fixed federal asset limits with MD adherence; QI has funding cap/priority queue; household size primarily individual/couple; annual reapplication required

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries (age 65+), those under 65 with disabilities, individuals nearing Medicare eligibility, caregivers, family members, and low-income individuals seeking financial help[1][2][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[1][2][3]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary or family/caregiver of one; available statewide in Maryland[1][2][3]

benefits.value: Free, confidential, unbiased one-on-one counseling, education, and advocacy including: Medicare plan comparison/enrollment (Parts A/B/C/D), Medigap, prescription drug coverage (Part D), Medicare Advantage, long-term care insurance, billing issues/appeals/denials/grievances, financial assistance programs (MSPs, Extra Help, Medicaid), preventive services, healthcare fraud/abuse detection, community workshops/presentations; no fixed dollar amounts or hours, provided via phone, in-person, or events[1][2][3][4]

methods: Phone: (410) 767-1100 or toll-free 1-800-243-3425 to connect to local SHIP office[3][5][7][9]
Website: https://aging.maryland.gov/pages/state-health-insurance-program.aspx (includes SHIP Directory for local offices)[3][5]
In-person or phone via local county offices (e.g., Carroll County: 410-386-3800, 125 Stoner Avenue, Westminster, MD 21157; Prince George's, Anne Arundel, etc.)[1][2][6]
No formal application form required; contact for counseling appointment[1][2][3]
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling available via phone or in-person; no specified processing delay[1][2][3]
forms: No SHIP-specific forms; for related financial aid: MSP Application (9705-DHS-QMB-SLMB-MSP), Extra Help (SSA i1020), Maryland SPDAP[6]
documents: No documents required for SHIP counseling itself; for assistance programs: Medicare card, income proof, may be requested during session[2][6]

geography.restrictions: None; covers all 23 counties and Baltimore City[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through local county offices/providers with same core services; contact central line for local office (e.g., Carroll, Prince George's, Anne Arundel, Montgomery, Frederick have dedicated pages)[1][2][6][8][9]
offices_or_providers: Maryland Department of Aging (central): 301 West Preston Street, Room 1007, Baltimore, MD 21201; local offices via https://aging.maryland.gov SHIP Directory[3][5]

gotchas: Not a financial aid or healthcare provider program—only free counseling/education, no direct payments or services; people confuse with Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) or Extra Help, which SHIP helps apply for but does not fund; services are free but require contacting local office as central line connects you; available to under-65 disabled Medicare beneficiaries and pre-eligibility individuals, often overlooked[1][2][3][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; counseling-only service via county network; open to Medicare families/caregivers without enrollment barriers[1][2][3]

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)

eligibility.income_limits:
Maryland SNAP eligibility is primarily based on income at or below 200% of the federal poverty level (FPL) for most households, with more flexible rules for those 60+ or disabled. For Oct 1, 2025–Sep 30, 2026 (relevant period): Elderly/disabled separate households use 165% FPL gross income limit. Full table from Maryland Hunger Solutions:

| Household Size | Max Gross Monthly Income (130% FPL) | Max Net Monthly Income (100% FPL) | Elderly/Disabled Separate Household (165% FPL) | Max Allotment |
|---------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $1,696 | $1,305 | $2,152 | $298 |
| 2 | $2,292 | $1,763 | $2,909 | $546 |
| 3 | $2,888 | $2,221 | $3,665 | $785 |
| 4 | $3,483 | $2,680 | $4,421 | $994 |
| 5 | $4,079 | $3,138 | $5,177 | $1,183 |
| 6 | $4,675 | $3,596 | $5,934 | $1,421 |
| 7 | $5,271 | $4,055 | $6,690 | $1,571 |
| 8 | $5,867 | $4,513 | $7,446 | $1,789 |
| Each additional | $596 | $459 | $757 | $218 |

Gross income includes Social Security, veterans' benefits, disability payments. Net income deducts expenses like medical costs for 60+ or disabled[2][6][1][7].
eligibility.asset_limits: Maryland has asset limits: Combined bank savings/checking under $2,001 (or under $3,001 if household includes person 60+ or disabled). Exempt: home value if owned, retirement savings, cash value of life insurance, income-producing property, household goods[5][1].
other_requirements: Maryland resident
U.S. citizen or lawfully present non-citizen
Social Security number for each household member
Able-bodied adults 16-60 (with exceptions) must register for work, accept suitable employment, participate in employment/training if referred (exceptions for 60+ or disabled)
Unemployed adults without children/disabilities limited to 3 months benefits every 3 years
Household includes those who live together and buy/prepare food together
Verification of income, child support, immigrant status, medical expenses (for 60+ or disabled)

benefits.value: EBT card (Maryland Independence Card) loaded monthly for SNAP-approved foods at grocery stores/farmers markets. Average ~$188/month for older adults; max allotments per table above (e.g., $298 for 1-person elderly/disabled). Maryland law (effective Oct 1) ensures seniors 62+ get at least $30/month (vs. federal $16 min)[2][3][6].

methods: Online: myDHR portal at dhs.maryland.gov (primary state site)
Phone: Call local Department of Social Services office (find via dhs.maryland.gov/local-offices) or Maryland SNAP hotline
Mail: Submit application to local DSS office
In-person: Local Department of Social Services offices statewide
application.processing_time: Must file application, complete interview, meet eligibility; some expedited processing available but typically 30 days[9][10].
forms: Maryland SNAP Application (DHS Form available via local DSS or online at dhs.maryland.gov)
documents: Proof of income (paystubs, employer letter, award letters for SS/VA/disability)
Proof of child support (court order, canceled checks)
Proof of legal immigrant status if applicable

geography.regional_variations: Administered by local Department of Social Services offices; potential differences in processing times or staff availability by county, but rules uniform[4][9].
offices_or_providers: Local DSS offices; find via dhs.maryland.gov/local-offices

gotchas: Include all who buy/prepare food together in household, even non-relatives
Seniors 60+ get deductions for medical expenses >$35/month, out-of-pocket costs
Work registration required unless 60+/disabled (but exceptions common for elderly)
Asset test applies (unlike some states); check bank balances carefully
3-month limit for able-bodied adults without dependents
Maryland boosts min benefit to $30 for 62+ (state-specific vs. federal)
Benefits calculated via net income formula for elderly/disabled: max allotment minus 30% net income[7]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income tables scale by household size with special 165% FPL gross limit for elderly/disabled separate households; state-specific $30 min benefit for 62+; asset test with elderly exception; uniform statewide but local DSS processing

programs.name: Senior Assisted Living Subsidy Program (SALS)
programs._raw:
{
  "name": "Senior Assisted Living Subsidy Program (SALS)",
  "eligibility": {
    "age": 62,
    "income_limits": "Net monthly income may not exceed 60% of the state median income (e.g., $4,358 per month or $52,200 per year for an individual as of recent county data; exact amount adjusts annually based on HUD state median income). Applies regardless of household size; no table for couples or larger households specified, but financial eligibility considers net income after deductions like $130/month personal allowance, medical expenses, and other costs. VA Aid & Attendance excluded from income calculation.[1][3][6]",
    "asset_limits": "No greater than $20,064 for singles or $26,400 for couples. What counts: standard countable assets (details not fully specified in sources). Exemptions: personal allowance deductions applied in income calc, but asset exemptions not detailed.[1][3][6]",
    "other_requirements": [
      "Resident in an assisted living facility licensed by Maryland Department of Health, or approved for entrance with a service agreement via Area Agency on Aging.",
      "Physically or mentally impaired, needing assistance with activities of daily living (e.g., bathing, grooming, dressing, mobility) provided by the facility.",
      "Net monthly income less than the approved monthly assisted living fee.",
      "Unless facility is nonprofit, applicant cannot be related to licensee (parent, child, spouse, aunt, uncle, niece, nephew, first cousin, in-law, partner, or officer).[5]",
      "Functional assessment required.[5]"
    ]
  },
  "benefits": {
    "type": "financial",
    "value": "Subsidy covers difference between resident's net monthly income (after $130 personal allowance deduction) and approved monthly assisted living fee. Supports specific services: meals, personal care (e.g., bathing, grooming, dressing), 24-hour supervision. Maximum monthly subsidy $1,000 in some areas (e.g., Carroll County).[1][6]",
    "varies_by": "region"
  },
  "application": {
    "methods": [
      "Contact local Maryland Access Point (MAP) office for eligibility screening, facility matching, and application assistance (e.g., Howard County MAP: (410)313-1234; Queen Anne's: 410-758-0848). In-person or phone primary routes.[1][3][8]",
      "No statewide online application or specific form name/number identified; process via local Area Agency on Aging/MAP.",
      "Assisted living facilities enrolled in program (must have 1+ year license, service agreement with Area Agency) assist with application.[3]"
    ],
    "processing_time": "Not specified statewide.",
    "waitlist": "Common; grant funding limited (e.g., active waitlists in Howard and Carroll Counties).[3][6]",
    "forms": ["No specific form name/number listed; financial and functional assessments conducted by MAP/Area Agency."],
    "documents": [
      "Proof of income (net monthly/annual, excluding VA Aid & Attendance).",
      "Proof of assets.",
      "Proof of age.",
      "Evidence of impairment/need for ADLs (functional assessment).",
      "Service agreement with participating assisted living facility.",
      "Medical expenses and other deductions for eligibility calc.[1][3][5]"
    ]
  },
  "geography": {
    "statewide": true,
    "restrictions": "Available only at assisted living facilities enrolled with local Area Agency on Aging and licensed by MD Dept. of Health.",
    "regional_variations": "Administered locally via Area Agencies on Aging/MAP offices; waitlists and max subsidy (e.g., $1,000 in Carroll) vary by county; participating facilities differ by region (any qualified facility may enroll).[3][6][7][8]",
    "offices_or_providers": ["Local Maryland Access Point (MAP) offices; Area Agencies on Aging; enrolled assisted living facilities by county (e.g., Howard, Carroll, Montgomery, Queen Anne's)."][1][3][6][7][8]
  },
  "official_source": "https://aging.maryland.gov/pages/senior-assisted-living-subsidy-program.aspx",
  "gotchas": [
    "Must already be resident or approved for entry into a participating facility with service agreement—cannot apply without facility commitment.[1][5]",
    "Income limit tied to 60% HUD state median (adjusts annually; check current via MAP).",
    "Assets capped low ($20k single/$26k couple); family relation restrictions if for-profit facility.[5]",
    "Waitlists common due to limited funding; not guaranteed.[3][6]",
    "Subsidy only bridges income-to-fee gap, not full coverage; max $1,000 in some areas.[6]",
    "Distinct from Medicaid waivers (e.g., different age/income).[2][4]"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "Income at 60% state median (annual HUD adjust); fixed asset caps by individual/couple only; requires pre-enrolled facility; regional waitlists/funding limits; no household size scaling beyond couple assets."
}

programs.name: National Family Caregiver Support Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits specified in program guidelines.[1][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits specified; no mention of asset counting or exemptions.[1][3][4]
other_requirements: Adult family members or other informal caregivers age 18 and older providing care to individuals 60 years of age and older
Adult family members or other informal caregivers age 18 and older providing care to individuals of any age with Alzheimer's disease and related disorders
Grandparents and other relatives (not parents) 55 years of age and older providing care to children under the age of 18
Grandparents and other relatives (not parents) 55 years of age and older providing care to adults age 18-59 with disabilities

benefits.value: Five specific services: 1) Information to caregivers about available services; 2) Assistance to caregivers in gaining access to services; 3) Individual counseling, organization of support groups, and caregiver training; 4) Respite care; 5) Supplemental services (on a limited basis). No fixed dollar amounts or hours per week specified; services are non-fee based where offered.[1][3][4][7]

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (administered by local network); statewide access via Maryland Department of Aging
No specific phone, URL, mail, or in-person details in results; eligibility screenings and application help provided by local providers (e.g., MAC Inc. for certain areas)
application.waitlist: Not specified; may vary locally due to limited supplemental services
forms: No specific form names or numbers identified
documents: Not specified; prepare proof of caregiver relationship and care recipient details for screening

geography.restrictions: Administered locally by network of Area Agencies on Aging; available across Maryland
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered by local providers (e.g., MAC Inc., Montgomery County programs); eligibility screenings and access may vary by local Area Agency on Aging; potential differences in availability of supplemental services or wait times by region
offices_or_providers: Local Area Agencies on Aging; examples include MAC Inc. and Montgomery County HHS

gotchas: No income or asset tests, but services like supplemental are limited basis—may not be available to all eligible
Must contact local Area Agency on Aging (not centralized state application); varies by region
Focuses on caregiver support services, not direct financial aid or healthcare to care recipient
Eligibility emphasizes informal/unpaid caregivers; professional caregivers may not qualify
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered locally by Area Agencies on Aging with no statewide income/asset tests; services fixed at five types but supplemental limited; eligibility has multiple caregiver categories beyond just elderly care

programs.name: Maryland Access Point (MAP)

eligibility.age: 50+ for older adults; 18+ for individuals with disabilities; any age for caregivers or those with concerns about someone in need[1]
eligibility.income_limits: No income limits — open to individuals of all income levels[1]
other_requirements: Residents of Maryland[4]
Experiencing long-term care needs or seeking information about aging/disability services[4]

benefits.value: Information and referral (free); Options Counseling (free, person-centered planning); in-home and community-based supports (caregiving, wellness checks, adult day care); social and educational programs; food access assistance (SNAP, meal distribution, congregate dining); health services navigation; assistance accessing private and public resources for long-term services and supports[1][3][4]
benefits.varies_by: Individual need and eligibility for underlying programs (MAP itself is free to all; underlying programs may have income/asset limits)

methods: Phone: 1-844-MAP-LINK (1-844-627-5465) — connects to 211 Maryland information specialist, who refers to local MAP office[4]
Local MAP office: Contact your county's MAP Coordinator directly (example: St. Mary's County at 301-475-4200 ext. 1057)[1]
Online needs assessment: marylandaccesspoint.211md.org/needsassessment/ (10-question tool to identify resources)[2]
In-person: Visit local MAP site to schedule appointment with Division of Home and Community-Based Services[1]
application.forms: No specific form names/numbers provided in available sources; needs assessment is conducted through counselor conversation or online tool[2]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources; all information and assistance is confidential[1]

geography.restrictions: Available statewide through 20 local Maryland Access Point sites[4]
geography.regional_variations: Each county has a local MAP office with its own coordinator and staff; services may vary slightly by county provider network (example: St. Mary's County partners with Health Department, Southern Maryland Center for Independent Living, Department of Social Services)[1][5]
geography.offices_or_providers: 20 local MAP sites across Maryland; specific locations available through 1-844-627-5465 or aging.maryland.gov[4]

gotchas: MAP itself is free and has no income limits, but it is primarily an information, referral, and counseling service — not a direct benefit program. Families should understand that MAP connects people to OTHER programs, which may have their own income/asset limits[1][4]
The statewide phone line (1-844-MAP-LINK) routes to 211 Maryland first, then to local MAP offices for complex needs — there is an intermediary step[4]
Eligibility for underlying programs (food assistance, health coverage, long-term care) is separate from MAP eligibility and must be assessed individually[2]
No specific processing times or waitlist information is publicly available — families should ask about timelines when they call[4]
MAP offers 'Options Counseling' as a distinct service for long-term care planning, but this requires referral from 211 or direct contact with local MAP office[4]
The online needs assessment tool (10 questions) is a self-guided starting point, but personalized counseling from a MAP counselor is recommended for comprehensive planning[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: MAP is a statewide information and referral hub with no income/asset limits and broad eligibility (age 50+, adults 18+ with disabilities, caregivers of any age). It is not a direct benefit program but a gateway to other services. Services are delivered through 20 local county-based offices, so availability and partner agencies vary by region. The program emphasizes person-centered Options Counseling and long-term care planning rather than direct financial or in-kind benefits. Underlying programs accessed through MAP may have their own eligibility criteria.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**MAP is a gateway, not a benefit.** The most important thing families need to understand is that Maryland Access Point is a free information and counseling service that connects people to other programs — it is not itself a direct benefit program[1][4]. When you call or visit MAP, you're getting help navigating what you might qualify for elsewhere.

**No income test for MAP itself.** Unlike many aging programs, MAP has no income limits[1]. Anyone can access it. However, the programs MAP refers you to (SNAP, Medicaid, housing assistance, etc.) will have their own eligibility requirements.

**Multiple entry points.** Families can start by calling 1-844-MAP-LINK, visiting their local county MAP office, or using the online 10-question needs assessment tool[2][4]. The phone line is the most accessible starting point for families unsure where to begin.

**Options Counseling is the core service.** Beyond referrals, MAP offers free person-centered counseling to help families develop a long-term care plan tailored to their loved one's needs, preferences, and values[4].

**Regional variation exists but is subtle.** While MAP operates in all 20 Maryland counties, each county's local office partners with different agencies (health departments, social services, disability centers)[1][5]. Families should ask their local MAP office what specific services are available in their area.

programs.name: Maryland Senior Call Check Program (SCC)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; program is free for all eligible Maryland residents[4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[1][2][4]
other_requirements: Maryland resident[1][2][4]
Has a working landline or cell phone (TTY available); no automated call blocking technology[1][2][3][4]
Must apply on their own behalf; no one else can apply for them[1][2][3]
Highly recommended to have a willing alternate contact person (e.g., adult child, neighbor, loved one)[1][2][3][4]

benefits.value: Daily automated telephone call or text message at a pre-selected time block (8 a.m. to 4 p.m., ideally within one-hour window chosen by participant, such as 8-10 a.m. or 2-4 p.m.). If unanswered after three attempts, calls alternate contact to encourage wellness check. If alternate also unreachable, contacts local non-emergency law enforcement for welfare check. Service is free and first-in-nation statewide program[1][3][4][6]

methods: Phone: Toll-free 1-866-50-CHECK (1-866-502-0560), lines open Monday-Friday 8 a.m.-5 p.m., Saturday 9 a.m.-3 p.m.[4]
Online: https://aging.maryland.gov/Pages/senior-call-check-sign-up.aspx[1]

Mail: Download paper application from https://aging.maryland.gov/siteassets/pages/senior-call-check/maryland-senior-call-check-paper-application.pdf and mail to address on form (Baltimore MD 21202)[1]
application.processing_time: Verification and enrollment typically within 24 hours Monday-Saturday; participant informed when daily calls begin[2][5]
forms: Maryland Senior Call Check Program Application (paper version available as PDF)[1][2]
documents: No specific documents required; provide name, county of residence, address, phone number, date of birth (implied), alternate contact name and phone, preferred call time block, and signature agreeing to terms[1][2][3]

geography.restrictions: None; available to any Maryland resident meeting eligibility[1][4][6]
geography.regional_variations: No regional differences noted; statewide program with local law enforcement welfare checks varying by locality only in execution (resources limited)[1][2]

gotchas: Must disable automated call blocking on phone and alternate's phone[1][2][4]
Participant must apply themselves; family cannot apply on their behalf[1][2][3]
No guaranteed timely welfare check by law enforcement due to limited resources[1][2]
Age eligibility listed variably as 60 or 65 across sources; official site states 60+[4]; older PDFs say 65[1][2][5]
Alternate contact highly recommended but not strictly required (program may assign one)[4]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income or asset test; simple eligibility based on age, residency, and phone access; fixed daily service statewide with no tiers or variations; self-application only

programs.name: Maryland Durable Medical Equipment Reuse Program (DME)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits. Program is available to all Maryland residents regardless of income.[2]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in available documentation.
other_requirements: Must be a Maryland resident[2]
Must have any illness, injury, or disability[2]

For complex DME equipment, Part C of the application must be completed by a healthcare professional (Physician, Physical Therapist, Occupational Therapist, Physician's Assistant, or Nurse Practitioner)[2]
No prescription or insurance required[2]

benefits.value: Free durable medical equipment. No cost to recipients.[2]

basic_dme_no_provider_documentation_need: Shower chairs and tub transfer benches[1]
Bedside commodes and toilet safety rails[1]
complex_dme_requires_healthcare_provider: Power wheelchairs (Physical or Occupational Therapist only)[1]
Power scooters (Physical or Occupational Therapist only)[1]
additional_available_items: Ramps (loaned for up to 120 days, fully ADA compliant)[4]

benefits.duration: Equipment provided to a beneficiary is theirs to keep permanently. Borrowing period is unlimited.[2][3]

methods: Online: Click 'Apply Online' button at https://onestop.md.gov/forms/basic-durable-medical-equipment-request-form-618bcb19e9b09f023f463960[1]
Email: [email protected][1]
Mail or in-person: 11701 Crain Highway, Cheltenham, MD 20623[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified in available documentation. After submission, form enters 'In-Review' status. DME staff will contact requester via preferred method to notify if equipment is in-stock and schedule pickup appointment.[1]
application.waitlist: Not explicitly mentioned. Availability depends on whether requested equipment is in-stock.[1]
forms: Basic DME Request Form (for basic equipment)[1]
Complex Request Form (Parts B and C) completed by healthcare provider (for complex equipment)[1]
documents: For basic DME: No healthcare provider documentation required[1]
For complex DME: Part C of application must be completed by treating healthcare professional (Physician, Physical Therapist, Occupational Therapist, Physician's Assistant, or Nurse Practitioner)[2]
All fields marked with '*' are required; failure to provide will void request[1]

geography.regional_variations: Multiple pickup locations exist around the state, but specific regional variations in availability or processing time are not documented.[1][2]
offices_or_providers: Primary office: 11701 Crain Highway, Cheltenham, MD 20623[1]
Multiple delivery/pickup locations around the state (specific locations not listed in search results)[1][2]

gotchas: Home delivery is NOT available. Equipment must be picked up by appointment only at designated pickup sites.[2]
Complex DME (wheelchairs, hospital beds, lifts, power equipment) requires healthcare provider documentation. Basic equipment does not.[1]
Always call ahead to verify specific equipment is in stock before applying, as availability varies.[3]
All fields marked with '*' on the form are required; incomplete applications will be voided.[1]
For power wheelchairs and power scooters, only Physical or Occupational Therapists can request these (not other healthcare providers).[1]
Program encourages returning equipment when no longer needed so it can help other residents.[2]
No prescription or insurance required, making this accessible to uninsured individuals.[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: This program has NO income or asset limits, making it universally accessible to Maryland residents with any illness, injury, or disability. The key distinction is between basic equipment (no provider documentation needed) and complex equipment (requires healthcare provider completion of Part C). Equipment is provided permanently, not loaned (except ramps, which have a 120-day loan period). The program is statewide but pickup is appointment-only at multiple locations. Processing timeline is not publicly specified, creating uncertainty for applicants about wait times.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**No financial barriers to access:** Unlike many assistance programs, Maryland's DME program has no income limits, asset limits, or insurance requirements.[2] An elderly person qualifies based solely on having an illness, injury, or disability and being a Maryland resident.

**Two-tier application process:** Basic equipment (walkers, canes, shower chairs, commodes, transfer boards) can be requested without healthcare provider involvement.[1] Complex equipment (wheelchairs, hospital beds, lifts) requires a healthcare provider to complete Part C of the application.[1] This distinction affects how quickly families can apply.

**Permanent ownership, not temporary loan:** Once equipment is provided, the beneficiary keeps it permanently.[2] This differs from many DME programs that loan equipment temporarily.

**Pickup-only model:** Families must pick up equipment by appointment at one of several locations around the state; home delivery is not available.[2] This is a significant logistical consideration for families with mobility-limited elderly relatives.

**Availability uncertainty:** The program does not publish processing timelines or equipment availability by region. Families are advised to call ahead (240-230-8000) to verify whether specific equipment is in stock before formally applying.[3]

**Healthcare provider requirement for complex items:** If an elderly person needs a wheelchair, hospital bed, or mechanical lift, their doctor, physical therapist, occupational therapist, physician's assistant, or nurse practitioner must complete Part C of the application.[2] This adds a step but is not a barrier if the person has an existing healthcare provider.

programs.name: Senior Care Services

eligibility.income_limits: Varies by program; for Medicaid LTSS like Community First Choice (CFC), single person up to $16,243/year (effective Jan 1, 2024); nursing home Medicaid single applicant contributes nearly all income with assets under $2,500 (2026); Medicaid Waiver for Older Adults at 300% SSI level; case-by-case if living with relative[1][2][5]. No full household size table specified statewide.
eligibility.asset_limits: Typically $2,000-$2,500 for individual (e.g., $2,500 for nursing home Medicaid single applicant in 2026; $2,000 for most under Waiver, $2,500 for some). Counts standard assets; exemptions not detailed in sources[2][5].
other_requirements: Maryland resident
US citizen or qualified alien
At risk of institutionalization or nursing facility level of care (NFLOC)
Physically or mentally impaired needing assistance with ADLs
For some: certified severe chronic disability or specific employment/education status (younger programs)

benefits.value: Case management, in-home personal care, household chores, transportation, adult day care, medical supplies, PERS, meal delivery, home modifications, assistive technology, medical day care, nursing, nurse monitoring. Gap-filling funds if available; slots limited (e.g., ICS: 100 slots)[1][3][6]. No fixed dollar amounts or hours specified.

methods: Phone: Aging and Disability Services Resource Unit at 240-777-3000 (Montgomery County example); Registry toll-free 1-866-417-3480 for Waiver[3][5]
In-person: Local Area Agency on Aging or during in-home assessment[1][3]
Registry for slots: Add name to Waiver Services Registry[5]
application.processing_time: State law requires decision within 30 days; varies by funding[5]
application.waitlist: Yes, via statewide Registry for Waiver slots (contacted by number in line, new slots July 1); varies by funding availability, not entitlement[3][5]
forms: Form 3871 for NFLOC determination sent to Delmarva Foundation[5]

geography.restrictions: Administered via local Area Agencies on Aging (AAAs); must contact local AAA
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through 24 local AAAs; wait times, funding, providers vary by county (e.g., Montgomery County specific process); eligibility review case-by-case if in relative's home[1][3]
offices_or_providers: Local Area Agencies on Aging; e.g., Montgomery County Aging and Disability Services

gotchas: Not an entitlement—grant-funded with limited slots and waitlists
Must join statewide Registry for Waiver; check position in line
Financial eligibility reviewed case-by-case if living with relative
Services not duplicative of facility-provided (e.g., assisted living residents eligible for gaps only)
NFLOC required; free medical eval via AERS
Over income/assets? Strategies exist like spend-down, but consult planner
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered statewide via 24 local AAAs with regional delivery variations; priority/waitlist by slots, not fixed benefits; ties to Medicaid LTSS waivers requiring NFLOC; no uniform income table by household size across programs
